title: A world in flux
content: In the early 1200s, Anatolia was shared between two weakening powers: the Christian Byzantine Empire and the Seljuk Sultanate of Rum. Both had once dominated the region, but centuries of war and political decay had worn them down. Into this unstable world, one small tribe would find the opening it needed.
next: Who were the people who would rise from this chaos?

title: The Kayı tribe
content: The Kayı were a branch of the Oguz Turks from the Central Asian steppes - nomadic warriors and skilled horsemen. Led by Ertugrul Gazi, they were swept westward by the Mongol advance and eventually settled near the town of Sogut in western Anatolia. Their new home sat on the border between the Seljuk Sultanate and the Byzantine Empire - a dangerous but strategically rich frontier.
next: Then the Mongols shattered everything around them.

title: The Mongol shockwave
content: In 1243, the Mongol army crushed the Seljuks at the Battle of Kose Dag. The defeat broke Seljuk power in Anatolia, leaving the region fragmented among dozens of small Turkish principalities called baiks. Each baik rushed to fill the vacuum. The Kayı - positioned on the lucrative Byzantine frontier - were well placed to grow. The same chaos that destroyed others was, for them, an opportunity.
next: What ideas would fuel this tribe's ambitions?

title: The Gazi warrior ethos
content: The frontier between Christian Byzantium and Muslim Anatolia had its own powerful ideology: the Gazi ethos. A Gazi was a holy warrior fighting to expand Islamic territory into Christian lands. This identity attracted fighters from across the region - not just Kayı tribesmen, but warriors from many backgrounds who saw conquest as both religious duty and opportunity. The Kayı could offer what others could not: a cause worth fighting for.
next: One leader would channel this energy into something lasting.

title: Osman takes the lead
content: Around 1280, Ertugrul's son Osman took command of the Kayı. He was ambitious and politically shrewd, understanding that military victories needed to be paired with governance. He did not just raid - he organized, attracted followers with success, and built alliances with neighboring leaders. A Muslim holy man named Sheikh Edebali became his father-in-law and spiritual advisor, lending religious legitimacy to his growing authority.
next: What vision did Osman claim would guide his dynasty?

title: Osman's dream
content: According to Ottoman tradition, Osman had a vision while staying with Sheikh Edebali: a great tree grew from his chest, its branches spreading to cover the entire world, its shade sheltering peoples of every background. Islamic scholars interpreted this as divine permission to rule a great empire. Whether historical or legendary, this story became a cornerstone of Ottoman identity - a mandate from heaven that inspired generations of rulers and subjects alike.
next: How did Osman turn vision into reality?

title: Independence declared
content: In 1299, Osman formally declared independence from the crumbling Seljuk Sultanate. This was not just symbolic defiance - it was a calculated move to position the Ottomans as the successor power in the region. By breaking away, Osman attracted followers who wanted to join a rising force rather than a declining one. The small frontier principality now had a name, a purpose, and a ruler who believed in both.
next: What did this new state actually look like?

title: Expanding the frontier
content: With independence came aggressive expansion into Byzantine territory. Osman's forces captured the towns of Bilecik, Yenisehir, and Inegol, securing strategic positions and new populations to govern. He established Yenisehir as his administrative center, revealing that he understood building an empire required organization as much as conquest. His forces were already diverse - Turkic nomads and Christian mercenaries fighting side by side under the Gazi banner.
next: What great prize did Osman fix his sights on?

title: The long siege of Bursa
content: Bursa was one of the wealthiest Byzantine cities in the region - a major trading hub protected by strong walls. Osman began besieging it but died in 1326 before the city surrendered. His last wish was to be buried in Bursa. His son Orhan fulfilled that request after the city fell. Osman's tomb became a pilgrimage site, and future sultans would be ceremonially invested at his grave - a living link between every ruler and the dynasty's founding spirit.
next: What did Orhan build from this inheritance?

title: From tribe to state
content: When Bursa fell in 1326, Orhan made it the first Ottoman capital. He issued the first Ottoman coin - the silver akce - standardizing trade and taxation across their territory. He built a central bureaucracy to govern diverse populations. Under Orhan, the Ottomans stopped being a warband and became a state with institutions, currency, and organized administration. This transformation was as important as any military victory.
next: How did the Ottomans build the army that would conquer Europe?

title: The Janissary Corps
content: One of the most consequential decisions of early Ottoman rule was the creation of the Janissary Corps through the Devsirme system. The Ottomans took young boys from Christian communities in conquered territories, converted them to Islam, and trained them from childhood as elite soldiers loyal directly to the sultan - not to any tribe or local lord. The Janissaries were paid, professional, and deeply disciplined. They became the backbone of the Ottoman army for the next two centuries.
next: How did Ottoman expansion continue under Orhan?

title: Capturing Byzantine Anatolia
content: Under Orhan, the Ottomans continued dismantling Byzantine power in Anatolia. In 1331, they captured Nicaea (Iznik), the ancient city where early Christians had defined core church doctrine - a profound symbolic blow to Byzantine prestige. In 1337, the port city of Nicomedia (Izmit) fell, giving the Ottomans access to the Sea of Marmara. Constantinople was now surrounded on the Asian side, and the Byzantines were running out of room.
next: Then came the moment that changed everything.

title: The leap into Europe
content: In 1354, an earthquake damaged the Byzantine fortifications at Gallipoli, the strategic peninsula controlling the passage between Europe and Asia. Ottoman forces seized the opportunity and occupied it. For the first time, the Ottomans had a permanent foothold on European soil. This was not a raid - it was a foundation. The Balkans lay open before them, and the history of Europe had just shifted.
next: Who led the drive deep into the European continent?

title: Murad I and the European turn
content: Murad I, who became sultan in 1362, understood that the empire's future lay in Europe. The Balkans were wealthy, politically fragmented, and full of rival states that could be picked off one by one. His campaigns pushed deep into southeastern Europe, and in 1369 he captured Adrianople - a major city that became the new Ottoman capital. Moving the capital to Europe declared the empire's intentions: this was no longer a frontier principality. It was a continental power.
next: How did the Ottomans manage the peoples they conquered?

title: The art of vassalage
content: Murad developed a shrewd approach to Balkan expansion. Rather than destroying every state he encountered, he often allowed local rulers to remain in power as vassals - paying tribute and providing troops while keeping their thrones. This was cheaper than outright conquest and reduced the risk of united resistance. Serbia, Walachia, and other Balkan kingdoms found themselves pulled steadily into Ottoman orbit without always understanding how tightly they were held.
next: But some refused to submit, and Murad crushed them openly.

title: Battle of Maritsa
content: In 1371, a coalition of Serbian nobles marched against Ottoman forces hoping to push them out of Europe. At the Battle of Maritsa, the Ottomans delivered a crushing defeat. The Serbian coalition was shattered and Ottoman dominance over southeastern Europe was firmly established. This battle is less famous than Kosovo but arguably more decisive - it broke organized Christian resistance in the Balkans and opened the way north.
next: The defining battle of Murad's reign was still to come.

title: Battle of Kosovo
content: In 1389, Serbian Prince Lazar assembled a coalition of Balkan forces for a final stand against Ottoman expansion. The two armies met at Kosovo Polje - the Field of Blackbirds. The battle was brutal and costly on both sides. In a shocking moment, Murad himself was assassinated by a Serbian knight who feigned surrender to get close to the sultan. Yet the Ottomans still won decisively. Serbian independence effectively ended that day, and Ottoman dominance in the Balkans was secured for generations.
next: With Murad dead on the battlefield, who came next?

title: Bayezid I - the Lightning Sultan
content: Bayezid I earned the nickname Yildirim - lightning - for the speed and ferocity of his campaigns. Where his predecessors had expanded methodically, Bayezid was relentless. He swept through Bulgaria, absorbed large parts of Serbia, and swallowed the remaining independent Turkish baiks in Anatolia. Within a decade, the empire had roughly doubled in size. But speed had a cost: he was stretching forces across two continents with few pauses to consolidate.
next: Bayezid then turned his eyes on the greatest prize in the world.

title: The siege of Constantinople
content: In 1394, Bayezid blockaded Constantinople. The Byzantine capital had shrunk to a shadow of its former self, but its legendary walls had never been breached. Bayezid's blockade slowly strangled the city for years. Inside, Emperor Manuel II sent desperate appeals to Western Europe. Small amounts of aid arrived - just enough to keep the city alive. The siege failed, but Bayezid had demonstrated that Constantinople was now trapped. The question was only when it would fall.
next: Europe tried to strike back. The results were disastrous.

title: Battle of Nicopolis - crusade crushed
content: In 1396, a massive European coalition - French knights, Hungarian cavalry, and soldiers from across Christendom - assembled for a new crusade to push the Ottomans back. They were confident and well-equipped. At the Battle of Nicopolis on the Danube, Bayezid's forces trapped and routed them. Thousands were killed or captured. The defeat shattered Western Europe's last serious attempt to roll back Ottoman power by military force before the fall of Constantinople.
next: Then a threat emerged that even Bayezid could not withstand.

title: Timur - the greater predator
content: Timur, known in the West as Tamerlane, had built a vast empire stretching from Persia to India. When Bayezid's rapid expansion pushed into territory Timur considered his sphere, conflict became inevitable. Timur was no Balkan prince or European crusader - he was a conqueror of Bayezid's own caliber, commanding a battle-hardened army. Crucially, many Anatolian baik leaders who resented Ottoman absorption defected to Timur's side, weakening Bayezid before the fighting even began.
next: The two empires collided at Ankara in 1402.

title: Battle of Ankara - catastrophe
content: At the Battle of Ankara in 1402, Bayezid's army was routed. The sultan himself - who had humiliated European crusaders and blockaded the greatest city in Christendom - was taken prisoner. He died in captivity the following year. Timur deliberately dismantled Ottoman power, restoring the baiks Bayezid had absorbed. The empire that had seemed unstoppable was suddenly fragmented. Watching from across the Bosphorus, Europe wondered if the Ottoman threat was finally over.
next: What happens when an empire loses its sultan and its unity?

title: The Ottoman Interregnum
content: Bayezid's four sons fought each other for eleven years in a civil war known as the Interregnum. Suleyman held the European provinces, Musa eventually challenged him there, and Mehmed controlled Anatolia. Former vassal states in the Balkans seized their independence. Timur's restored baiks resisted reunification. The treasury drained, trade collapsed, and ordinary people suffered. The empire that had conquered from the Adriatic to the Euphrates teetered on the edge of extinction.
next: One brother would pull it back from the brink.

title: Mehmed I restores the empire
content: Mehmed I, the most capable of Bayezid's sons, emerged victorious in 1413 with Byzantine support. He spent his reign patiently reassembling what the civil war had shattered - negotiating with baik leaders, coaxing Balkan vassals back into submission, and repairing the administrative systems that had been destroyed. He avoided unnecessary provocations and rebuilt the Janissary corps. When he died in 1421, the empire had been rescued from the verge of collapse.
next: His son inherited the throne and immediately faced fresh threats.

title: Murad II consolidates
content: Murad II took power in 1421 and immediately suppressed a Byzantine-backed rebellion. He then launched sustained campaigns throughout the Balkans, pushing into Serbia, Walachia, and Albania. In Albania, a renegade Ottoman-trained commander named Scanderbeg switched sides and led a fierce guerrilla resistance that frustrated Ottoman forces for decades - a reminder that even a great empire had limits in mountainous terrain. But elsewhere Murad systematically rebuilt Ottoman power.
next: Europe made one last organized attempt to stop the Ottomans.

title: Battle of Varna - the last crusade
content: In 1444, a crusading army of Polish and Hungarian forces marched into the Balkans hoping to drive the Ottomans back. Murad had briefly stepped aside in favor of his young son Mehmed II, but the crisis forced him to return to command. At the Battle of Varna, Ottoman forces destroyed the crusader army. The Hungarian king Wladyslaw III died in the fighting. This was the last serious Western European military attempt to stop Ottoman expansion in the Balkans - and it failed completely.
next: Murad's son was already planning something far greater.

title: The young sultan's obsession
content: When Mehmed II became sultan in 1451 at age nineteen, he had one overriding goal: Constantinople. The Byzantine Empire had been reduced to the city itself and a few scattered territories, but it still stood as a symbol of everything the Ottomans had not yet conquered. More practically, the city sat astride the vital route between Europe and Asia, and as long as it stood, enemies had a rallying point against Ottoman power. Mehmed planned with meticulous obsession.
next: What weapons would break walls that had stood for a thousand years?

title: Engineering the impossible
content: Mehmed made two decisive preparations. First, he ordered the construction of Rumeli Hisari, a massive fortress on the European shore of the Bosphorus, completed in just four months. Its cannons could stop any ship, cutting Constantinople off from Black Sea supply routes. Then he hired a Hungarian engineer named Orban to cast the largest cannons ever built - some over eight meters long, hurling stone balls weighing 600 kilograms. Constantinople's ancient walls had been designed to stop armies, not artillery of this scale.
next: In April 1453, the siege began.

title: 53 days
content: Constantinople's walls had repelled attackers for over a thousand years. When Mehmed's army surrounded the city in April 1453, Emperor Constantine XI commanded perhaps 7,000 defenders against an Ottoman force of 50,000 to 80,000. For 53 days the great cannons battered the walls while defenders patched the damage each night. Desperate appeals for Western reinforcement went unanswered. The city was isolated, its supplies shrinking, its walls increasingly shattered - but still the defenders held on.
next: How did the walls finally break?

title: The fall of Constantinople
content: In the early hours of May 29, 1453, Ottoman troops breached the walls and poured into the city. Emperor Constantine XI reportedly threw aside his imperial regalia and died fighting in the streets - his body was never found. By nightfall it was over. The Byzantine Empire, a continuation of the Roman Empire that had endured for over a thousand years, ceased to exist. For the Islamic world it was a triumph. For Christendom, a profound shock that reverberated for generations.
next: What did Mehmed do with history's greatest prize?

title: A new imperial capital
content: Mehmed moved immediately to rebuild Constantinople, now renamed Istanbul. He encouraged immigration from across the empire, bringing in artisans, scholars, and merchants to repopulate the emptied city. He converted the Hagia Sophia - the greatest cathedral of Eastern Christianity - into a mosque. Yet in a display of pragmatic tolerance, he protected the city's Christian and Jewish communities, understanding that their skills and networks were essential to the city's revival. This combination of imperial ambition and relative religious tolerance would define Ottoman rule for centuries to come.
next: Test what you remember.
